title: Built an AI system that autonomously trades Polymarket 5-min crypto prediction markets. Streaming it live.
author: JrichCapital
selftext:
Claude estimates probabilities, Kelly Criterion sizes bets, executes via CLOB API. No manual intervention — runs 24/7 on a VPS.

\~70% win rate on BTC/ETH. The edge exists only in the 50-70¢ price band — above that, the market is already efficient.

Stack: Python asyncio + Preact/TS + Polymarket CLOB + Binance feeds.

Live stream: youtube @nadirlabsai

Happy to answer architecture questions.

comments.author: Calm_Produce7945
comments.body: With the library, did you create the linear graph that moves? I don't want to use TradingView...

replies.author: JrichCapital
replies.body:
The main charts use TradingView Lightweight Charts it’s free, open source, and honestly the easiest way to get a clean financial chart running. About 30 lines of code to get a live-updating line chart with crosshair and price markers.
But the equity curves, the PnL heatmaps, and the hourly performance bars are all native SVG drawn manually. No library. Just <svg>, <path>, and <rect> with some math for scaling. Lighter, faster, and you control every pixel.
If you want a moving line chart without TradingView, you have two solid options:
Canvas API draw your own. Keep an array of points, clear the canvas each frame, draw the line with quadratic CurveTo for smooth curves. Add a gradient fill below. It’s more work but zero dependencies. That’s how our terminal charts started before we switched.
D3.js more powerful, steeper learning curve, but great for anything interactive. Overkill for a simple live line though.
Honestly TradingView Lightweight Charts is not the same as the full TradingView embed. It’s a standalone 40KB library with no branding, no watermark, no iframe. Just a clean chart you fully control. Worth reconsidering if your concern is the heavy TradingView widget.​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​
